name: Unit Checking
description: Making sure your answers have the right units

content.overview: Unit checking is your first defense against silly mistakes. Before celebrating your answer, ask: 'Does this have the right units?' {{viz:diagram-01}}
content.explanation:
Unit checking is simple: look at your final answer and verify the units make sense for what you calculated.

Example: You calculate speed and get 50 m/s. Good! Speed should be in m/s (distance/time).

But if you calculate speed and get 50 meters, that's WRONG. Speed isn't distance—it's distance per time.

The trick: {{viz:diagram-02}}

1. Know what units your answer SHOULD have
2. Check what units you actually got
3. If they match → probably right
4. If they don't match → definitely wrong

**Example: Wrong answer caught by unit checking** {{viz:anim-01}}

You calculate: F = m × a = 5 kg × 3 m/s² = 15 kg·m/s²

You know force should be in Newtons (N), which equals kg·m/s². Your answer checks out! ✓

But imagine you accidentally wrote: F = m + a = 5 kg + 3 m/s² = ??? 

You can't add kg and m/s²! Units don't match. This tells you something went wrong. {{viz:chart-01}}

Unit checking won't tell you if your answer is 100% correct, but it WILL catch obvious blunders.
keyPoints: Know what units your answer should have
Check what units you actually got
If units don't match, something is wrong
If units match, your answer is at least plausible
Do this check EVERY TIME you solve a problem
content.whyItMatters: Unit checking saves you from embarrassment. It's the fastest way to catch if you used the wrong formula, made an algebra mistake, or forgot a conversion.
